title: Bond pricing and yield to maturity

questions.prompt: A bond's coupon rate is 5% and its YTM is 7%. The bond is trading at:
questions.explanation: When the market demands a higher yield than the coupon offers, the price must drop below face so total return matches YTM. c < y → discount.

questions.prompt: A 5-year bond with face value $1,000, 6% annual coupon, YTM 5%. What is its price? Answer in dollars to the nearest cent.
A 10-year bond has modified duration of 7.5. If yields jump 50 bps (from 5.0% to 5.5%), approximately what happens to price?
questions.explanation: ΔP/P ≈ −D_mod × Δy = −7.5 × 0.005 = −3.75%. The negative sign means price falls when yield rises.

questions.prompt: Why was interest-rate risk such a problem for Silicon Valley Bank in 2023?
choices: They had short-duration assets and long-duration liabilities
They had long-duration assets that lost mark-to-market value when yields spiked in 2022-2023, eroding their capital base
They held no Treasuries at all
Their assets and liabilities were perfectly matched
questions.explanation: A classic duration mismatch: long-duration assets (Treasuries, MBS) fell sharply in price as yields rose, while deposit liabilities did not appreciate to offset.

furtherReading.title: The Silicon Valley Bank Collapse: Takeaways and Lessons Learned
furtherReading.why: UNC finance faculty piece that uses SVB to show exactly how duration and YTM math destroyed a bank — the canonical real-world bond-pricing case.
